summary: Dan Shapiro revisits a set of ten AI principles he wrote in June 2023, just six months after ChatGPT launched, and evaluates how well they held up over two years. His rules cover model improvement velocity, provider lock-in risks, hosted vs. self-hosted models, multimodality, and the critical importance of validated data. He finds most predictions proved accurate, with minor adjustments on fine-tuning and context windows. The overarching message is that despite AI's breakneck pace of change, certain strategic principles about avoiding lock-in, staying current, and valuing ground truth remain durable.
key_points: Core AI models improve faster than any company can innovate around them, making prompt-engineering startups vulnerable to being absorbed by model improvements
No single model or provider maintains durable leadership — the top spot rotates constantly between GPT, Claude, Gemini, and others
Validated, ground-truth data is the most strategically durable asset in AI, more valuable than any model or technique
RAG and embeddings will persist despite growing context windows because real-world data always exceeds token limits
The AI landscape demands avoiding long-term commitments to any vendor, model, or integration approach
money_quotes: Don't invest too much time in making the state of the art perform better. The state of the art will perform better on its own.
Startups keep springing up whose core benefit is 'prompt/finetune AI to make it better at ___'. They keep getting eaten by the models just getting better all by themselves.
This is not a place to get attached to any one model or model provider.
Validated, ground-truth data is the greatest asset in the AI world. LLMs know the patterns; you want to anchor them in the facts.
Garbage in, garbage out, no matter how good the technology gets.
The vaporware used to just be saying 'AI!' and maybe having some machine learning feature that was half a decade old. Now businesses are integrating LLMs — badly. Buyer beware.
It's a great time to be experimenting and innovating, but a bad time to be making long-term technology purposes.
key_insight: Despite AI's rapid evolution, the most durable strategic principles are about what to avoid — lock-in, long-term bets, and trusting unvalidated data — rather than what specific technology to adopt.
